behaviors.description: The request sent to the external API always includes the complete list of valid taxonomy labels.
behaviors.justification: Failure is at a module handoff boundary (taxonomy config -> request builder -> API client). A unit test of only the formatter can miss wiring defects where the sent payload drops labels. Real network is not required; an in-process fake transport can assert the actual outbound request body.
behaviors.test_description: Wire the classifier module with a fake API transport, invoke classify() with a known taxonomy, and assert the captured outbound payload contains every configured label exactly once before dispatch.

behaviors.description: When the API returns a label that is not in the taxonomy, the module returns "unknown" instead of forwarding the unexpected value to the caller.
behaviors.justification: This behavior depends on interaction between API response handling and taxonomy validation. A pure unit test of validator logic is insufficient for the stated failure mode ('API returns ... module returns ...'). No real OS/network or model-quality dependency is required; stubbed API response is enough.
behaviors.test_description: Run classifier with a fake API client returning an out-of-taxonomy label (for example "medical"); assert returned value is "unknown" and no raw unexpected label is exposed.

behaviors.description: For identical raw API responses, the module's normalization step always produces the same final label.
behaviors.justification: Behavior text scopes to normalization determinism for identical inputs, which is local function/object logic with no required module handoff, I/O, or model dependence.
behaviors.test_description: Call normalization function repeatedly with the same raw response fixture (case/whitespace variants already fixed in fixture) and assert identical canonical label output each run.

behaviors.description: On repeated live API calls for the same document and taxonomy, the returned label remains consistent unless the provider response itself changes.
behaviors.justification: Behavior explicitly requires live API calls and consistency of model-produced labels, which depends on real AI output characteristics and possible non-determinism. Integration stubs would hide this failure. Not Workflow because this is repeated single-step classification, not a multi-agent pipeline.
behaviors.test_description: Against the real provider, submit the same document+taxonomy multiple times, record raw provider outputs and normalized labels, and assert label stability whenever raw provider response is unchanged.

behaviors.description: Request construction enforces the configured token budget before dispatch (for example truncate/reject according to policy).
behaviors.justification: Failure appears at handoff between token counting/policy logic and dispatch gate ('before dispatch'). A unit test of token math alone can miss wiring where oversize requests are still sent. Real provider/network is unnecessary for this behavior; fake dispatch boundary suffices.
behaviors.test_description: Configure a small token budget, pass an over-budget document, and assert module applies configured policy (truncate or reject) and only dispatches a compliant request (or no request on reject).

behaviors.description: Provider-reported token usage for a typical document stays within the configured per-call budget.
behaviors.justification: This requires a real AI provider call and real provider token accounting. Mocked/stubbed usage values can always be made compliant and can hide real overruns caused by provider-side tokenization/packing behavior. Rejected Workflow: behavior is a single model call, not multi-step agent sequencing.
behaviors.test_description: Run classification on representative documents against the real provider, collect provider-reported usage fields, and assert observed per-call token usage is <= configured budget with alert/failure on any exceedance.

behaviors.description: When the API signals that the request rate is too high, the module waits and retries rather than returning an error to the caller.
behaviors.justification: Core failure is orchestration across retry policy, backoff timing, and API client response handling. In-process fake API + controllable clock can reproduce it; real network/OS behavior is not strictly required. Correctness does not depend on model output content, so Agentic is not required.
behaviors.test_description: Use a fake API client that returns rate-limit error then success; run classifier with fake clock/sleeper and assert module performs wait/backoff, retries expected count, and returns successful label instead of surfacing initial error.
